name: Stuxnet
description: A sophisticated worm that sabotaged Iranian nuclear centrifuges by exploiting multiple Windows zero-days and Siemens controllers.
remediation: Patching, isolating industrial networks, updating antivirus and intrusion detection for ICS.
impact: First demonstrated that malware could cause physical destruction; shifted ICS cybersecurity to the top of safety agendas.
realWorldExample: Imagine a factory machine being tricked into breaking itself because the control software was secretly altered.
rootCause: Insufficient air-gapped/security controls on industrial control systems and shared USB usage.
discovery: Detected after centrifuge anomalies were observed; forensic analysis linked to Stuxnet code.
mitigationNotes: Segment ICS networks, enforce removable media controls, apply vendor patches.

name: WannaCry
description: Ransomware spread quickly via the EternalBlue Windows vulnerability and encrypted user files demanding Bitcoin payment.
remediation: Emergency patching (MS17-010), disable SMBv1, full backups, incident response guided recovery.
impact: Affected 200,000+ devices in 150+ countries, including UK hospitals, causing canceled appointments and lost hours.
realWorldExample: Like a digital locker that instantly locks every computer in a hospital, stopping doctors from accessing patient records.
losses: $4B+ global impact, healthcare and logistics downtime
rootCause: Unpatched legacy Windows servers with exposed SMBv1 and weak patch management.
discovery: Burst of infections in May 2017; kill switch domain accidentally discovered slowed propagation.
detectionTimeline: 24 hours to initial detection, weeks to global patch roll-out.
mitigationNotes: Disable SMBv1, apply MS17-010, isolate infected hosts, restore backups.

name: NotPetya
description: Originally disguised as ransomware, this destructive malware wiped disks and made recovery extremely hard.
remediation: Complete system rebuilds, hardened credentials, segmented networks and strengthened disaster recovery.
impact: Estimated $10B+ in damage worldwide; impacted shipping, pharmaceuticals, energy and manufacturing.
realWorldExample: A business losing every digital record overnight and being unable to restart operations for days.
attackVector: Compromised Ukrainian accounting software update (M.E. Doc) with EternalBlue lateral movement
rootCause: Insecure software supply chain plus weak lateral movement defense.
discovery: Detected by Ukrainian CERT and global partners after unusual malware behavior.
detectionTimeline: Minutes-to-hours for local infection; days for global awareness.
mitigationNotes: Prioritize supply chain audits, network segmentation, and rapid communication channels.

name: SolarWinds Orion
description: Supply-chain compromise where attackers inserted malware into a trusted network management update.
remediation: Credential revocation, reinstallation of systems, multi-factor authentication, supply chain audit.
impact: Breached U.S. government agencies and 18,000 organizations, changing global software trust practices.
realWorldExample: Like getting a poisoned update through a vendor you trust and then allowing thieves inside your entire house.
attackVector: Supply-chain trojanized SolarWinds Orion software update
losses: Hundreds of millions in response costs and asset rebuilds
rootCause: Insecure build pipeline and insufficient code integrity verification in third-party vendor updates.
discovery: Discovered by FireEye in December 2020 during its own breach investigation.
detectionTimeline: Weeks active before discovery; months of clean-up and incident response.
mitigationNotes: Implement binary signing checks, two-person code review, and transparency in CI/CD.

name: Equifax
description: Personal data breach exposing 147M people's details, due to unpatched Apache Struts vulnerability.
remediation: Better patching, encryption at rest, monitoring, consumer credit protection programs.
impact: Massive identity risk; led to $700M settlement, stronger privacy laws in US and abroad.
realWorldExample: Imagine your full identity records being copied by thieves, then used to open fake bank accounts in your name.
attackVector: Unpatched Apache Struts CVE-2017-5638 in web application
losses: $1.4B+ in breach response, fines, and litigation
rootCause: Failure in patch management and vulnerability scanning on externally facing web assets.
discovery: Detected after faked file upload attempts and abnormal traffic patterns.
detectionTimeline: Months of exposure before detection; breach lasted several weeks.
mitigationNotes: Automate patching, establish vulnerability disclosure program, restrict admin access.

name: Marriot-Starwood
description: Unauthorized database access stole personal data of 500M hotel guests over several years.
remediation: Revocation of compromised credentials, decommissioning affected systems, customer notifications.
impact: Global privacy loss; heavy regulatory fines due to GDPR violations.
realWorldExample: Like an intruder reading your travel history, phone number, and passport details for years undetected.
attackVector: Long-running unauthorized access to backend reservation database (mostly unencrypted data)
losses: Estimated $28M in fines plus business reputational harm

name: Target POS breach
description: Hackers stole 40M credit/debit card numbers via compromised point-of-sale malware.
perpetrator: Criminal group linked to Eastern Europe
remediation: EMV payments, network segmentation, security audit and monitoring.
impact: Cost $162M after insurance; re-emphasized PCI compliance and supply chain security.
realWorldExample: Like electronic thieves copying your card data with a hidden device on a checkout scanner.
attackVector: Compromised HVAC vendor credentials and POS malware deployment
losses: $162M+ and tens of millions of card replacements

name: Colonial Pipeline
description: DarkSide ransomware knocked down major US fuel pipeline, causing regional gasoline shortages.
remediation: Shut down systems, restored from backups, enhanced OT security, announced cyber defense policies.
impact: Panic buying, price rises, motivated US executive order on cybersecurity.
realWorldExample: A hacker forcing a fuel company to stop delivery, leading to empty gas stations and long lines.
attackVector: Ransomware access via compromised VPN credentials and lack of MFA

name: Yahoo breach
description: All 3 billion Yahoo accounts exposed via credential theft and unpatched server vulnerabilities.
remediation: User password resets, increased security, delayed disclosure criticized.
impact: Loss of trust; reduced acquisition value of Yahoo to Verizon.
realWorldExample: If your email password, personal questions, and account info are handed to criminals.
attackVector: Stolen session cookies and forged requests due to inadequate account security
losses: Potential billions in value reduction for company; identity fraud impact unknown

name: Adobe ColdFusion
description: Zero-day vulnerability in Adobe ColdFusion exploited to deploy ransomware across corporate servers.
remediation: Patch distribution, affected vendor notifications, and security posture improvements.
impact: Massive global email provider outages and customer disruptions, highlighting patch urgency.
realWorldExample: A hole in the software provider's wall lets attackers break into many houses at once.
attackVector: Zero-day remote code execution in web platform
losses: Hundreds of millions in incident response and downtime

name: Microsoft Exchange ProxyLogon
description: Four zero-day vulnerabilities in Exchange Server allowed remote attackers to access mailboxes and deploy malware.
remediation: Urgent Microsoft patch, forced updates, hunting for backdoors, and incident response across thousands of organizations.
impact: Hundreds of thousands of servers were compromised; a major wake-up call for patching internet-facing systems.
realWorldExample: Like a thief opening the front door of many businesses because a manufacturer forgot to lock it.
attackVector: Microsoft Exchange remote code execution zero-days (ProxyLogon)
losses: Over $100M in enterprise response and remediation
affected: Hundreds of thousands of Exchange servers

name: PayPal Business Logic Abuse
description: Attackers used business process design flaws to withdraw funds or bypass authentication without exploiting a vulnerability codebase.
remediation: Hold button design review, strict transaction monitoring, and additional business logic validation.
impact: Showed that secure code without process validation still fails; non-CVE business logic risk is real.
realWorldExample: Like a cashier being able to ring up an item for $0 by using an unplanned button sequence.
attackVector: Business process logic gap in transaction state flow
losses: Variable fraud losses, possibly low millions due to quick mitigation

name: Google Docs Phishing Campaign
description: A widespread email phishing campaign impersonated Google Docs to steal OAuth tokens and get access to victim mailboxes.
remediation: Emergency OAuth consent revocation, user education, phishing detection alerts, multi-factor authentication.
impact: Millions of accounts potentially exposed; forced credential/token revocation on Google and increased vigilance.
realWorldExample: Like receiving an invitation to a shared file from someone you know, clicking it, and then giving a malicious service access to your email.
attackVector: Email phishing + rogue OAuth token consent
losses: Data exposure and credential theft risk; financial loss indirect
